prompt_md:
QA's offline eval over their golden dataset reports **94% accuracy** for an AI feature using an LLM-as-judge, so the team ships. After launch, users complain specifically about answers in one question category. Investigation shows that category made up only a tiny fraction of the golden dataset, and when humans re-rate those answers they disagree sharply with the judge, which had rated long, confident-but-wrong answers as acceptable.

**Decision:** What should the team do? **Reason:** Why does that address the root cause?

decision_choices: Stratify the dataset to represent the failing category, slice eval scores by segment, and validate the judge against human ratings
Trust the 94% aggregate and dismiss the complaints as anecdotal
Switch to a larger model and assume every category improves
Remove the failing category from the dataset so the score stays high
reason_choices: Because a high aggregate hid a failing subgroup and a biased judge over-rated it, so representative data, per-segment scoring, and judge validation restore a trustworthy measurement
Because aggregate scores are always more reliable than individual user reports
Because more randomness in outputs will average out the failing category
Because a smaller dataset is faster to run in CI and that is what matters most
